q: A 32-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation presents with a blood pressure of 148/96 mmHg on two separate readings 4 hours apart. Urinalysis shows no proteinuria. Which diagnosis is most appropriate?
rationale: Gestational hypertension is defined as new-onset hypertension (BP >= 140/90) after 20 weeks gestation without proteinuria or other signs of preeclampsia. The absence of proteinuria and onset after 20 weeks distinguishes this from preeclampsia and chronic hypertension.

q: Which of the following is a risk factor for developing gestational hypertension?
rationale: Obesity with a BMI greater than 30 is a well-established risk factor for gestational hypertension. Other risk factors include nulliparity (not multiparity), age over 35, and multiple gestation.

q: A patient diagnosed with gestational hypertension at 37 weeks asks the nurse when she can expect to deliver. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Delivery should be delayed until 42 weeks to ensure fetal maturity.
You will be induced only if you develop preeclampsia.
Delivery is typically recommended at 37 weeks for gestational hypertension without severe features.
rationale: ACOG recommends delivery at 37 weeks gestation for women with gestational hypertension without severe features. Delaying delivery beyond this point increases maternal and fetal risks without significant benefit.

q: The nurse is monitoring a patient with gestational hypertension. Which finding would indicate progression to preeclampsia?
options: Proteinuria of 350 mg in a 24-hour urine collection
Maternal weight gain of 1 pound per week
rationale: Proteinuria of 300 mg or greater in a 24-hour urine collection indicates progression from gestational hypertension to preeclampsia. The other options are either within normal limits or consistent with gestational hypertension alone.

q: A nurse is teaching a patient with gestational hypertension about home monitoring. Which instruction is most important?
options: Check blood pressure once a week at the same time.
Report any sudden weight gain, headaches, or visual changes immediately.
Strict bed rest is required until delivery.
Increase sodium intake to maintain blood pressure.
rationale: Patients with gestational hypertension should be instructed to report warning signs of disease progression such as sudden weight gain (edema), headaches, and visual disturbances, which may indicate worsening to preeclampsia.

q: Which laboratory test is NOT routinely part of the initial workup for a patient newly diagnosed with gestational hypertension?
rationale: Hemoglobin A1c is used for diabetes screening and is not part of the routine workup for gestational hypertension. CBC, liver function tests, and serum creatinine are all part of the standard evaluation to assess for end-organ damage and rule out preeclampsia or HELLP syndrome.

q: A patient at 34 weeks gestation with gestational hypertension has a blood pressure reading of 162/108 mmHg. What is the priority nursing action?
options: Recheck the blood pressure in 24 hours.
Notify the healthcare provider immediately for possible severe-range hypertension.
Administer acetaminophen for the elevated blood pressure.
Encourage the patient to rest on her right side.
rationale: A blood pressure of 160/110 mmHg or higher is considered severe-range hypertension and requires immediate notification of the healthcare provider. Antihypertensive treatment should be initiated promptly to prevent complications such as stroke.

q: A patient with gestational hypertension asks why bed rest is no longer universally recommended. Which response by the nurse is most accurate?
options: Bed rest has been shown to worsen hypertension in pregnancy.
Bed rest is only recommended in the third trimester.
Research has not demonstrated that strict bed rest improves outcomes and it increases the risk of thromboembolism.
Activity restriction has been proven to prevent progression to preeclampsia.
rationale: Current evidence does not support strict bed rest for gestational hypertension as it has not been shown to improve maternal or fetal outcomes. Additionally, prolonged bed rest increases the risk of venous thromboembolism.

q: Which patient is at highest risk for developing gestational hypertension?
options: A 25-year-old multigravida with no prior hypertensive history
A 30-year-old with a history of uncomplicated prior pregnancies
A 22-year-old with a singleton pregnancy and normal BMI
A 38-year-old nullipara with a BMI of 34 and a family history of preeclampsia
rationale: Advanced maternal age (over 35), nulliparity, obesity, and family history of preeclampsia are all significant risk factors for gestational hypertension. The combination of these factors in this patient places her at highest risk.

q: A patient at 38 weeks with gestational hypertension is being prepared for induction. The nurse explains that the primary reason for delivery at this time is to:
options: Prevent maternal seizures that are imminent.
Allow the patient to start antihypertensive medications postpartum.
Reduce the risk of progression to more severe hypertensive disease.
Ensure the baby reaches full term before the placenta fails.
rationale: Delivery is the definitive treatment for gestational hypertension. At 37-38 weeks, the benefits of delivery in reducing the risk of progression to preeclampsia or other complications outweigh the risks of continued expectant management.

q: Which statement by a patient with gestational hypertension indicates a need for further teaching?
options: I will monitor my blood pressure at home twice daily.
My blood pressure will definitely return to normal after delivery.
I should report a headache that does not go away with rest.
I need to keep all my prenatal appointments for close monitoring.
rationale: While gestational hypertension typically resolves within 12 weeks postpartum, it is not guaranteed. Some women may develop chronic hypertension. The statement that blood pressure will 'definitely' return to normal requires further education.

q: The nurse notes that a patient with gestational hypertension has 1+ pitting edema in both lower extremities. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Document the finding and continue monitoring as mild dependent edema can be normal in pregnancy.
rationale: Mild dependent edema in the lower extremities is common in pregnancy and is not by itself a sign of worsening disease. The nurse should document the finding and continue monitoring for other signs of progression such as proteinuria or worsening hypertension.

q: A patient with gestational hypertension has a non-stress test (NST) that is reactive. This result indicates:
options: The fetus is in distress and needs immediate delivery.
The mother's blood pressure has returned to normal.
The fetus is well-oxygenated with adequate placental perfusion at this time.
rationale: A reactive NST demonstrates fetal heart rate accelerations in response to movement, indicating adequate oxygenation and placental function. It is a reassuring finding in the management of gestational hypertension.

q: When does gestational hypertension, by definition, first develop?
rationale: Gestational hypertension is defined as new-onset hypertension occurring after 20 weeks of gestation in a woman who was previously normotensive. Hypertension diagnosed before 20 weeks is classified as chronic hypertension.

q: A 28-year-old at 35 weeks gestation is diagnosed with preeclampsia. Her blood pressure is 152/98 mmHg with 2+ proteinuria on dipstick. She reports no headache, visual changes, or epigastric pain. Her labs are normal. This presentation is consistent with:
rationale: Preeclampsia without severe features is characterized by BP >= 140/90 mmHg with proteinuria, in the absence of severe features such as BP >= 160/110, thrombocytopenia, elevated liver enzymes, renal insufficiency, pulmonary edema, or cerebral/visual symptoms.

q: Which set of findings meets the diagnostic criteria for preeclampsia with severe features?
options: BP 145/95 mmHg, trace proteinuria, platelet count 200,000/mcL
BP 168/112 mmHg, 3+ proteinuria, platelet count 85,000/mcL, elevated AST
BP 138/88 mmHg, no proteinuria, normal labs
BP 140/90 mmHg, 1+ proteinuria, normal liver enzymes
rationale: Preeclampsia with severe features includes BP >= 160/110, thrombocytopenia (platelets < 100,000), elevated liver enzymes, and significant proteinuria. This patient meets multiple criteria for severe features.

q: A patient at 30 weeks with preeclampsia with severe features asks why she is receiving betamethasone injections. The nurse explains that this medication is given to:
options: Accelerate fetal lung maturity in case early delivery is needed.
rationale: Betamethasone is a corticosteroid given to promote fetal lung maturity when preterm delivery (before 34 weeks) is anticipated. In preeclampsia with severe features, early delivery may be necessary, making fetal lung maturation a priority.

q: HELLP syndrome is characterized by which triad of findings?
options: Hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelet count
Headache, epigastric pain, low blood pressure
Hyperreflexia, elevated creatinine, low urine output
rationale: HELLP syndrome stands for Hemolysis (H), Elevated Liver enzymes (EL), and Low Platelet count (LP). It is a severe complication of preeclampsia that can be life-threatening and typically requires prompt delivery.

q: A nurse reviews labs for a patient suspected of having HELLP syndrome. Which result supports this diagnosis?
options: LDH of 750 IU/L with schistocytes on peripheral smear
rationale: An elevated LDH (> 600 IU/L) with schistocytes (fragmented red blood cells) on peripheral smear indicates hemolysis, a hallmark of HELLP syndrome. Normal platelet count, normal AST, and normal bilirubin would not support the diagnosis.

q: A patient at 28 weeks is diagnosed with preeclampsia without severe features. The plan includes expectant management. Which nursing intervention is most important during this period?
options: Restrict the patient to a clear liquid diet.
Monitor for signs of disease progression including worsening BP, labs, and symptoms.
Prepare the patient for immediate cesarean section.
Discontinue all fetal monitoring to reduce maternal stress.
rationale: Expectant management of preeclampsia without severe features before 37 weeks requires vigilant monitoring for signs of progression to severe disease, including serial blood pressures, laboratory assessments, and symptom evaluation.

q: Which symptom reported by a patient with preeclampsia should prompt the nurse to suspect progression to severe disease?
options: Mild ankle swelling at the end of the day
Persistent severe headache unrelieved by acetaminophen
rationale: A persistent severe headache unresponsive to treatment is a warning sign of severe preeclampsia, possibly indicating cerebral edema or impending eclampsia. This requires immediate evaluation and intervention.

q: A patient with preeclampsia at 34 weeks reports sudden right upper quadrant pain. The nurse suspects this may indicate:
options: Hepatic involvement consistent with severe preeclampsia or HELLP syndrome
rationale: Right upper quadrant or epigastric pain in a patient with preeclampsia suggests hepatic capsule distension from liver edema or hemorrhage, which is a feature of severe preeclampsia and HELLP syndrome requiring urgent evaluation.

q: Which protein-to-creatinine ratio on a spot urine sample is significant for preeclampsia diagnosis?
rationale: A urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of 0.3 mg/mg or greater correlates with 300 mg of protein in a 24-hour collection and is considered significant for the diagnosis of preeclampsia.

q: A patient at 36 weeks with preeclampsia without severe features has stable labs and blood pressures. When should delivery be planned?
options: At 40 weeks gestation to optimize fetal weight.
Only after the onset of spontaneous labor.
rationale: For preeclampsia without severe features, ACOG recommends delivery at 37 weeks gestation. Expectant management beyond 37 weeks does not improve outcomes and increases the risk of complications.

q: A patient with preeclampsia has a platelet count of 90,000/mcL. The nurse recognizes that this finding:
options: Indicates thrombocytopenia and is a criterion for severe features.
Is within normal limits for pregnancy.
Suggests the patient has gestational diabetes.
rationale: A platelet count below 100,000/mcL is thrombocytopenia and constitutes a severe feature of preeclampsia. This finding increases the risk of bleeding complications and may necessitate prompt delivery.

q: Which visual symptom in a preeclamptic patient is most concerning?
options: Mild blurring when reading fine print
Mild sensitivity to bright lights in the morning
rationale: Scotomata and flashing lights indicate retinal arteriolar vasospasm or cortical irritability, which are severe features of preeclampsia and may herald impending eclamptic seizures.

q: In preeclampsia, the primary pathophysiology involves:
options: Abnormal placental implantation leading to endothelial dysfunction and vasospasm.
rationale: Preeclampsia is believed to originate from abnormal placentation and defective spiral artery remodeling, leading to placental ischemia, release of antiangiogenic factors, systemic endothelial dysfunction, and widespread vasospasm.

q: A patient at 29 weeks with preeclampsia with severe features has stable maternal and fetal status. The healthcare provider elects expectant management. The nurse understands the goal is to:
options: Delay delivery to allow the preeclampsia to resolve on its own.
Avoid the use of magnesium sulfate.
Gain additional time for fetal maturation while closely monitoring for deterioration.
Wait until 40 weeks for a full-term delivery.
rationale: In carefully selected patients with severe preeclampsia before 34 weeks at tertiary centers, expectant management aims to prolong pregnancy to improve fetal maturity while providing close surveillance for maternal or fetal deterioration that would necessitate delivery.

q: A 26-year-old patient at 39 weeks gestation has a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. She was previously diagnosed with preeclampsia. The nurse's first action should be:
options: Ensure patient safety by positioning on her side, protecting from injury, and calling for help.
rationale: During an eclamptic seizure, the immediate priority is patient safety. The nurse should turn the patient on her side to prevent aspiration, protect her from injury, maintain a patent airway, call for help, and prepare for magnesium sulfate administration.

q: Eclampsia is defined as:
options: Blood pressure greater than 200/120 mmHg during pregnancy.
New-onset tonic-clonic seizures in a patient with preeclampsia, not attributable to other causes.
Any seizure occurring during pregnancy regardless of etiology.
Loss of consciousness without seizure activity in preeclampsia.
rationale: Eclampsia is specifically defined as the new onset of generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with preeclampsia that cannot be attributed to other neurological conditions. It is a life-threatening obstetric emergency.

q: The drug of choice for the treatment and prevention of eclamptic seizures is:
rationale: Magnesium sulfate is the first-line agent for both the prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures. It is superior to other anticonvulsants including diazepam and phenytoin for this indication, as demonstrated by the Magpie trial.

q: What is the standard loading dose of magnesium sulfate for eclampsia prophylaxis/treatment?
rationale: The standard loading dose of magnesium sulfate is 4-6 grams administered intravenously over 15-20 minutes, followed by a maintenance infusion of 1-2 grams per hour. This achieves therapeutic serum levels to prevent seizures.

q: A patient receiving magnesium sulfate for eclampsia prevention has a respiratory rate of 10 breaths per minute. The nurse should:
options: Continue the infusion as the respiratory rate is normal.
Administer a bolus dose of magnesium sulfate.
Increase the infusion rate to achieve higher therapeutic levels.
Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion and notify the provider immediately.
rationale: Respiratory depression (rate < 12 breaths/min) is a sign of magnesium toxicity. The nurse must immediately stop the infusion and notify the provider. Calcium gluconate should be readily available as the antidote.

q: During an eclamptic seizure, which position should the nurse place the patient in?
rationale: The left lateral decubitus position helps prevent aspiration of secretions, maintains airway patency, and promotes uteroplacental blood flow by relieving aortocaval compression. This is the preferred position during and after an eclamptic seizure.

q: After an eclamptic seizure is controlled, the nurse should anticipate that the healthcare provider will likely recommend:
options: Discharge home with outpatient follow-up in 2 weeks.
Stopping magnesium sulfate and starting oral calcium supplements.
Delivery of the fetus, as eclampsia is an indication for delivery regardless of gestational age.
rationale: Eclampsia is an absolute indication for delivery after maternal stabilization, regardless of gestational age. The only definitive cure for eclampsia is delivery of the fetus and placenta.

q: A patient has a second eclamptic seizure despite receiving a magnesium sulfate infusion. What should the nurse anticipate?
options: Administration of an additional 2-gram IV bolus of magnesium sulfate.
Discontinue magnesium sulfate and start phenobarbital.
rationale: If a seizure recurs during magnesium sulfate therapy, an additional 2-gram bolus of magnesium sulfate may be administered IV over 3-5 minutes. The serum magnesium level should be checked to ensure it is within the therapeutic range (4-7 mEq/L).

q: The therapeutic serum magnesium level for seizure prophylaxis in eclampsia is:
rationale: The therapeutic range for magnesium sulfate in eclampsia prevention is 4-7 mEq/L. Levels below this range may be ineffective for seizure prophylaxis, while levels above 7-10 mEq/L increase the risk of toxicity including respiratory depression and cardiac arrest.

q: Eclampsia can occur at which of the following times?
options: During pregnancy, labor, or up to 6 weeks postpartum
Only during the third trimester of pregnancy
rationale: Eclampsia can occur antepartum, intrapartum, or postpartum (typically within 48 hours but up to 6 weeks after delivery). Late postpartum eclampsia, though less common, is a recognized entity.

q: Which assessment finding in a patient with eclampsia would be most concerning for impending cardiac arrest?
rationale: A serum magnesium level of 15 mEq/L is severely toxic and associated with cardiac arrest. Normal therapeutic levels are 4-7 mEq/L. Levels above 10-12 mEq/L cause respiratory paralysis, and levels above 15 mEq/L can cause cardiac arrest.

q: A nurse is caring for a postpartum patient who had eclampsia. How long should magnesium sulfate typically be continued after delivery?
options: It should be stopped immediately after delivery.
rationale: Magnesium sulfate is typically continued for 24-48 hours postpartum to prevent postpartum eclamptic seizures, as the risk of seizures persists in the immediate postpartum period.

q: During an eclamptic seizure, the fetal heart rate monitor shows prolonged decelerations. The nurse understands that:
options: Fetal bradycardia commonly occurs during eclamptic seizures and often resolves after the seizure ends; maternal stabilization is the priority.
This requires immediate cesarean section during the seizure.
The fetal heart rate pattern is unrelated to the seizure.
This is a normal fetal response and requires no monitoring.
rationale: Transient fetal bradycardia and late decelerations commonly occur during eclamptic seizures due to uterine hypertonus and decreased placental perfusion. These typically resolve within 3-10 minutes after the seizure ends. Maternal stabilization should take priority, and the fetal heart rate should be reassessed after recovery.

q: A 35-year-old woman has a documented history of hypertension diagnosed at age 30, before her pregnancy. She is now 16 weeks pregnant. Her condition is classified as:
rationale: Chronic hypertension in pregnancy is defined as hypertension that was present before pregnancy or diagnosed before 20 weeks gestation. This patient had a pre-existing diagnosis of hypertension, making this chronic hypertension.

q: Which antihypertensive medication is considered first-line for chronic hypertension management during pregnancy?
rationale: Labetalol is a first-line antihypertensive for use in pregnancy. ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) and angiotensin receptor blockers (losartan) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenic effects including fetal renal damage.

q: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy because they can cause:
options: Fetal renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, and fetal death
rationale: ACE inhibitors are teratogenic and can cause fetal renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios (decreased amniotic fluid), pulmonary hypoplasia, growth restriction, and fetal death. They must be discontinued before or early in pregnancy.

q: A patient with chronic hypertension in pregnancy has consistently well-controlled blood pressures on methyldopa. At what blood pressure threshold should the nurse be concerned about inadequate perfusion to the fetus if the BP is lowered too aggressively?
rationale: While the exact threshold varies, excessively low blood pressure (below approximately 80/50 mmHg) may compromise uteroplacental perfusion. The goal in treating chronic hypertension in pregnancy is to prevent severe hypertension while maintaining adequate organ and placental perfusion.

q: A pregnant patient with chronic hypertension controlled on nifedipine is at risk for which complication specific to chronic hypertension in pregnancy?
rationale: Chronic hypertension in pregnancy increases the risk of placental abruption due to chronic vascular damage to the placental bed. Other risks include fetal growth restriction, preterm delivery, and superimposed preeclampsia.

q: The nurse is teaching a patient with chronic hypertension about pregnancy-specific risks. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Chronic hypertension has no additional risks during pregnancy.
Women with chronic hypertension have a 25-50% risk of developing superimposed preeclampsia.
Chronic hypertension always converts to gestational hypertension during pregnancy.
Chronic hypertension resolves during pregnancy due to the normal physiologic decrease in blood pressure.
rationale: Women with chronic hypertension have a significantly elevated risk (approximately 25-50%) of developing superimposed preeclampsia. This risk necessitates close monitoring throughout pregnancy.

q: Which fetal surveillance test is commonly ordered for a patient with chronic hypertension to assess for fetal growth restriction?
options: Serial ultrasound measurements for fetal growth
rationale: Serial ultrasound assessments are used to monitor fetal growth in pregnancies complicated by chronic hypertension, as impaired uteroplacental perfusion can lead to intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR).

q: A patient with chronic hypertension is currently taking atenolol. During preconception counseling, the nurse should advise that:
options: Atenolol is the preferred beta-blocker in pregnancy.
All antihypertensives should be stopped during pregnancy.
No medication changes are needed during pregnancy.
Atenolol should be switched to labetalol or nifedipine as it has been associated with fetal growth restriction.
rationale: Atenolol has been associated with fetal growth restriction when used in pregnancy and is not a preferred agent. Labetalol and nifedipine are safer alternatives. Preconception counseling should include transitioning to a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive.

q: Which finding would differentiate chronic hypertension from gestational hypertension in a patient presenting at 22 weeks gestation with elevated blood pressures?
options: Documentation of hypertension before 20 weeks gestation or pre-pregnancy
rationale: The key differentiator is timing: chronic hypertension is present before pregnancy or diagnosed before 20 weeks gestation, while gestational hypertension develops after 20 weeks. Documentation of pre-pregnancy or early pregnancy hypertension establishes the chronic diagnosis.

q: Low-dose aspirin (81 mg daily) is recommended for pregnant women with chronic hypertension beginning at what gestational age?
rationale: ACOG and USPSTF recommend initiating low-dose aspirin (81 mg/day) at 12-16 weeks gestation for women at high risk for preeclampsia, including those with chronic hypertension. This has been shown to reduce the incidence of preeclampsia.

q: A 30-year-old woman with chronic hypertension controlled on methyldopa presents at 32 weeks with new-onset proteinuria, a blood pressure of 158/104 mmHg, and a platelet count of 95,000/mcL. The most likely diagnosis is:
rationale: This patient with pre-existing chronic hypertension has developed new proteinuria, worsening blood pressure, and thrombocytopenia. The combination of chronic hypertension with new-onset preeclamptic features (proteinuria and severe-range BP with low platelets) defines superimposed preeclampsia with severe features.

q: Superimposed preeclampsia is defined as:
options: Preeclampsia that develops in a patient with no prior history of hypertension.
Preeclampsia that occurs only in the postpartum period.
The development of preeclampsia features (new proteinuria, severe features) in a woman with pre-existing chronic hypertension.
Hypertension that develops after 40 weeks gestation.
rationale: Superimposed preeclampsia occurs when a woman with chronic hypertension develops new features of preeclampsia, including new-onset proteinuria, sudden worsening of hypertension, or other signs of end-organ damage such as thrombocytopenia or elevated liver enzymes.

q: Which finding in a patient with chronic hypertension would most strongly suggest the development of superimposed preeclampsia?
options: Blood pressure remaining at baseline levels
New-onset proteinuria after 20 weeks with previously negative protein studies
rationale: The development of new-onset proteinuria after 20 weeks in a patient with previously negative protein assessments strongly suggests superimposed preeclampsia, particularly when combined with other signs such as worsening hypertension.

q: A patient with chronic hypertension and well-controlled blood pressure suddenly develops blood pressures of 170/110 mmHg along with elevated liver enzymes. This suggests:
options: Normal blood pressure variation in pregnancy
rationale: A sudden, significant increase in blood pressure to severe range along with new end-organ damage (elevated liver enzymes) in a patient with chronic hypertension indicates superimposed preeclampsia with severe features, which requires urgent management.

q: The risk of superimposed preeclampsia is increased in patients with chronic hypertension who also have which comorbidity?
rationale: Chronic kidney disease significantly increases the risk of superimposed preeclampsia in women with chronic hypertension due to pre-existing renal vascular damage and impaired renal function.

q: When managing superimposed preeclampsia with severe features at 30 weeks, the nurse should anticipate:
options: Administration of corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity and evaluation for delivery timing.
rationale: At 30 weeks with severe features, the management includes administering corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, starting magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, and evaluating the timing of delivery based on maternal and fetal condition.

q: A patient with superimposed preeclampsia at 35 weeks has worsening proteinuria but stable blood pressures and labs. The provider decides on delivery. The nurse explains that delivery is recommended because:
options: After 34 weeks with superimposed preeclampsia, the risks of continuing the pregnancy often outweigh the benefits of further fetal maturation.
The worsening proteinuria alone is dangerous enough to deliver immediately at any gestational age.
Delivery should only occur if the patient requests it.
Worsening proteinuria is not a concern in pregnancy.
rationale: For superimposed preeclampsia, delivery is generally recommended at 34-37 weeks depending on severity. At 35 weeks with worsening disease, continuing pregnancy risks maternal complications while the fetus has a good prognosis with delivery.

q: Superimposed preeclampsia can be difficult to diagnose because:
options: Blood pressure in chronic hypertension is always normal.
Preeclampsia does not occur in patients over 30 years old.
Patients with chronic hypertension may already have baseline proteinuria and elevated BP, making it hard to identify new changes.
Superimposed preeclampsia has no laboratory findings.
rationale: Diagnosing superimposed preeclampsia is challenging because patients with chronic hypertension may have pre-existing proteinuria and elevated blood pressures. Clinicians must look for worsening of these baseline values or the development of new features like thrombocytopenia or elevated liver enzymes.

q: Which additional monitoring is recommended for a patient with superimposed preeclampsia compared to chronic hypertension alone?
options: Increased frequency of fetal surveillance and serial laboratory assessments
rationale: Superimposed preeclampsia requires more frequent fetal surveillance (such as twice-weekly NSTs and amniotic fluid assessments) and serial laboratory monitoring (CBC, liver function, renal function) to detect disease progression.

q: A patient with chronic hypertension is at 24 weeks. Her blood pressure increases from a baseline of 140/90 to 165/110 mmHg, and she develops a severe headache. A 24-hour urine shows 500 mg protein (previously negative). What is the priority intervention?
options: Administer magnesium sulfate, initiate antihypertensive therapy, give corticosteroids, and evaluate for delivery.
Schedule a follow-up appointment in 1 week.
rationale: This patient has superimposed preeclampsia with severe features (severe-range BP, symptoms, new proteinuria). Management includes magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis, acute antihypertensive treatment, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity at 24 weeks, and evaluation for delivery timing.

q: The nurse is administering the loading dose of magnesium sulfate (4 grams IV). Which assessment is essential during the infusion?
options: Monitor blood pressure, respiratory rate, and deep tendon reflexes.
Measure fundal height during the infusion.
rationale: During magnesium sulfate administration, essential assessments include monitoring blood pressure (for hypotension), respiratory rate (depression indicates toxicity), and deep tendon reflexes (absent reflexes indicate elevated magnesium levels). Urine output should also be monitored.

q: A nurse assesses absent deep tendon reflexes in a patient receiving magnesium sulfate. This finding indicates:
options: The medication is at a sub-therapeutic level.
Magnesium toxicity; the infusion should be held and the provider notified.
The patient needs an increased dose.
rationale: Loss of deep tendon reflexes occurs at magnesium levels of approximately 7-10 mEq/L, indicating toxicity. The infusion should be stopped immediately and the healthcare provider notified. Calcium gluconate should be at the bedside.

q: What is the antidote for magnesium sulfate toxicity?
rationale: Calcium gluconate (1 gram IV given slowly over 3 minutes) is the antidote for magnesium sulfate toxicity. It antagonizes the effects of magnesium at the neuromuscular junction and myocardium, restoring cardiac conduction and neuromuscular function.

q: The dose of calcium gluconate as an antidote for magnesium toxicity is:
options: 1 gram IV administered slowly over 3 minutes
rationale: Calcium gluconate 1 gram (10 mL of a 10% solution) is administered IV slowly over 3 minutes to reverse magnesium toxicity. Rapid administration can cause cardiac arrhythmias.

q: Labetalol is administered IV for acute severe hypertension in preeclampsia. The nurse should monitor for which common side effect?
rationale: Labetalol is a combined alpha- and beta-adrenergic blocker. The beta-blocking effects can cause bradycardia, and the alpha-blocking effects can cause orthostatic hypotension and dizziness. The nurse should monitor heart rate and blood pressure closely.

q: Hydralazine is used for acute hypertension in pregnancy. Its mechanism of action is:
options: Blocking calcium channels in the myocardium
rationale: Hydralazine is a direct-acting arteriolar vasodilator that reduces peripheral vascular resistance. It has been used for decades in the acute management of severe hypertension in pregnancy.

q: A patient receiving IV hydralazine for severe preeclampsia develops a heart rate of 120 bpm. The nurse understands that this is likely due to:
options: Reflex tachycardia from vasodilation-induced drop in blood pressure
rationale: Reflex tachycardia is a common side effect of hydralazine due to the baroreceptor response to sudden blood pressure reduction from arteriolar vasodilation. The nurse should monitor the heart rate and report persistent tachycardia.

q: Nifedipine is used as an oral antihypertensive in pregnancy. It belongs to which drug class?
rationale: Nifedipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that works by inhibiting calcium influx into vascular smooth muscle cells, causing vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is considered safe and effective for use in pregnancy.

q: A concern when administering nifedipine concurrently with magnesium sulfate is:
options: Potentiation of hypotension and neuromuscular blockade
rationale: Both nifedipine and magnesium sulfate have vasodilatory and neuromuscular blocking effects. When used concurrently, there is a risk of potentiated hypotension and enhanced neuromuscular blockade, requiring close monitoring.

q: The maintenance dose of magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis is typically:
rationale: The standard maintenance dose of magnesium sulfate is 1-2 grams per hour by continuous IV infusion. This maintains therapeutic serum levels of 4-7 mEq/L for seizure prophylaxis.

q: A patient is receiving labetalol IV. The initial dose is typically 20 mg, and subsequent doses can be escalated to a maximum single dose of:
rationale: IV labetalol for acute severe hypertension in pregnancy typically starts at 20 mg, with escalating doses of 40 mg and 80 mg at 10-minute intervals if blood pressure is not controlled, up to a maximum single dose of 80 mg and a cumulative maximum of 300 mg.

q: Which assessment finding would indicate that calcium gluconate administration was effective in treating magnesium toxicity?
options: Return of deep tendon reflexes and improved respiratory rate
rationale: Effective calcium gluconate administration reverses the neuromuscular blocking effects of magnesium toxicity, resulting in return of deep tendon reflexes and improvement in respiratory rate and effort.

q: The nurse is preparing to administer IV magnesium sulfate. Which equipment must be at the bedside?
options: Calcium gluconate, suction equipment, and oxygen
rationale: Calcium gluconate (the antidote for magnesium toxicity), suction equipment, and supplemental oxygen must be at the bedside during magnesium sulfate administration. An infusion pump and continuous monitoring equipment are also essential.

q: Methyldopa is sometimes used for chronic hypertension in pregnancy. Its primary mechanism of action is:
options: Central alpha-2 adrenergic agonism that reduces sympathetic outflow
rationale: Methyldopa is a centrally acting alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that reduces sympathetic nervous system outflow, resulting in decreased peripheral vascular resistance and blood pressure. It has the longest track record of safety in pregnancy.

q: A nurse is caring for a patient with severe preeclampsia receiving magnesium sulfate. The minimum acceptable urine output is:
rationale: A minimum urine output of 30 mL/hour (or approximately 0.5 mL/kg/hour) is required to ensure adequate renal clearance of magnesium. Oliguria increases the risk of magnesium accumulation and toxicity because magnesium is renally excreted.

q: Which sign of magnesium toxicity would appear first as serum levels rise?
rationale: As magnesium levels rise, the first clinical sign of toxicity is loss of deep tendon reflexes (at approximately 7-10 mEq/L), followed by respiratory depression (10-12 mEq/L), and ultimately cardiac arrest (>15 mEq/L). This is why regular DTR assessment is critical.

q: A nurse prepares the room for a patient with severe preeclampsia. Which seizure precaution is most important?
options: Placing the bed in the highest position for easy access
Placing hard suction catheters at the bedside only
Keeping the room brightly lit for better assessment
Padding the side rails, dimming lights, and minimizing stimulation
rationale: Seizure precautions for preeclampsia include padding side rails to prevent injury, dimming lights, and minimizing environmental stimulation (noise, visitors, bright lights) that could trigger seizures. Suction equipment and oxygen should also be available.

q: The nurse is monitoring fetal heart tones in a patient with severe preeclampsia. Which fetal heart rate pattern is most concerning?
options: Recurrent late decelerations with minimal variability
Fetal heart rate of 140 bpm with early decelerations
rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with minimal variability indicate uteroplacental insufficiency with inadequate fetal oxygenation. This is a Category III fetal heart rate tracing that requires immediate intervention and may necessitate emergent delivery.

q: A patient with preeclampsia at 32 weeks asks why she has an indwelling urinary catheter. The nurse explains that it is placed to:
options: Accurately measure hourly urine output to monitor renal function and magnesium clearance.
Reduce the frequency of bathroom trips for patient comfort.
rationale: An indwelling catheter allows accurate hourly measurement of urine output, which is essential for monitoring renal function in preeclampsia and ensuring adequate magnesium clearance during magnesium sulfate therapy. Output should be at least 30 mL/hour.

q: The nurse notes that a patient with severe preeclampsia has 3+ pitting edema of the face and hands with rapid weight gain of 5 pounds in 2 days. This finding suggests:
options: Pathologic fluid retention and increased capillary permeability associated with severe preeclampsia.
A need to increase physical activity.
rationale: Rapid weight gain with facial and hand edema in preeclampsia indicates significant fluid shift from intravascular to extravascular spaces due to endothelial damage and increased capillary permeability, a hallmark of severe preeclampsia pathophysiology.

q: Which delivery method is preferred for a patient with preeclampsia?
options: Cesarean section is always required for preeclampsia.
Vaginal delivery is preferred unless obstetric indications for cesarean exist.
Vacuum-assisted delivery is the only safe option.
Delivery method does not matter in preeclampsia.
rationale: Vaginal delivery is generally preferred in preeclampsia as it is associated with less blood loss and faster recovery. Cesarean section is reserved for standard obstetric indications such as non-reassuring fetal status, failed induction, or malpresentation.

q: A nurse is caring for a patient with HELLP syndrome who is about to deliver. Which nursing consideration is critical related to her platelet count of 45,000/mcL?
options: Platelet count is adequate for any procedure.
The patient should receive aspirin to prevent further platelet consumption.
Regional anesthesia (epidural/spinal) may be contraindicated due to the risk of epidural hematoma.
rationale: A platelet count below 70,000-100,000/mcL (depending on institutional guidelines) is a contraindication for neuraxial anesthesia (epidural/spinal) due to the risk of epidural or spinal hematoma. General anesthesia may be required.

q: After delivery, the nurse continues to monitor a patient who had severe preeclampsia. The nurse understands that postpartum monitoring is important because:
options: Preeclampsia can worsen in the first 48-72 hours postpartum before improving.
Blood pressure always normalizes immediately after delivery.
Postpartum monitoring is not necessary once the placenta is delivered.
The risk of complications ends as soon as the baby is born.
rationale: Blood pressure in preeclampsia may initially worsen in the first 48-72 hours postpartum due to fluid mobilization and redistribution. Postpartum eclampsia can occur during this period, necessitating continued monitoring and magnesium sulfate therapy.

q: A patient at 34 weeks with severe preeclampsia asks why she cannot go home and come back when she is in labor. The best nursing response is:
options: You can go home if you promise to rest.
Severe preeclampsia requires continuous monitoring because maternal and fetal conditions can deteriorate rapidly, and delivery may need to happen urgently.
Going home is fine as long as you check your blood pressure daily.
The doctor is just being overly cautious.
rationale: Severe preeclampsia is a condition that can rapidly progress to life-threatening complications including eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, placental abruption, and stroke. Continuous inpatient monitoring allows for early detection and intervention.

q: The nurse educates a patient about the importance of kick counts in the management of preeclampsia. This is done to:
options: Assess fetal well-being, as decreased fetal movement may indicate compromised placental perfusion.
Determine if the patient is in labor.
rationale: Fetal kick counts are a simple tool for maternal assessment of fetal well-being. In preeclampsia, reduced fetal movement may indicate decreased placental perfusion and fetal compromise, warranting further evaluation such as a non-stress test or biophysical profile.

q: A nurse admits a patient at 28 weeks with a new diagnosis of preeclampsia with severe features. In what order should the nurse prioritize interventions?
options: Treat severe-range blood pressure, then draw laboratory tests, then discuss birth plan preferences.
Administer corticosteroids, then start magnesium sulfate, then treat blood pressure.
Start magnesium sulfate, then treat severe-range blood pressure, then administer corticosteroids.
Order a dietary consult, then start magnesium sulfate, then administer corticosteroids.
rationale: The priority is to prevent eclamptic seizures (magnesium sulfate), then treat severe-range hypertension (to prevent stroke), and then administer corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity. These interventions should be initiated as quickly as possible, often simultaneously.

q: A patient receiving magnesium sulfate reports feeling flushed and warm. The nurse should:
options: Stop the infusion immediately as this indicates an allergic reaction.
Increase the infusion rate to overcome the side effect.
Reassure the patient that flushing is a common side effect of magnesium sulfate due to vasodilation.
rationale: Flushing and a sensation of warmth are common side effects of magnesium sulfate caused by its vasodilatory properties. These are expected effects, not signs of toxicity or allergy, and typically do not require intervention other than patient reassurance.

q: Which nursing assessment takes the highest priority when caring for a patient on a magnesium sulfate infusion?
options: Continuous respiratory rate monitoring, deep tendon reflexes, and urine output measurement
rationale: The three critical assessments during magnesium sulfate therapy are respiratory rate (>= 12/min), deep tendon reflexes (should be present), and urine output (>= 30 mL/hr). These are the primary indicators of magnesium levels and toxicity risk.

q: A patient with gestational hypertension at 35 weeks has a biophysical profile (BPP) score of 4/10. The nurse should anticipate:
options: Repeating the BPP in one week.
Preparation for delivery due to the non-reassuring fetal status.
Discharging the patient with routine follow-up.
Administering tocolytics to prevent preterm labor.
rationale: A BPP score of 4/10 or less is non-reassuring and indicates possible fetal compromise. In the setting of gestational hypertension at 35 weeks, this finding typically warrants delivery to prevent further fetal deterioration.

q: Which patient statement indicates understanding of preeclampsia warning signs to report?
options: I will report if I feel the baby hiccup.
I should come in only for my scheduled appointments.
I need to call if I see spots or flashing lights in my vision.
Swelling in my feet at the end of the day means I have preeclampsia.
rationale: Visual disturbances such as scotomata (blind spots), flashing lights, or blurred vision are warning signs of worsening preeclampsia and potentially impending eclampsia. Patients should be taught to report these symptoms immediately.

q: The nurse is reviewing a patient's chart and notes the following lab values: LDH 650 IU/L, AST 85 U/L, platelets 80,000/mcL. These findings are most consistent with:
rationale: These lab values demonstrate the triad of HELLP syndrome: elevated LDH indicating hemolysis (H), elevated AST indicating liver damage (EL), and low platelet count (LP). This is a severe complication of preeclampsia requiring urgent intervention.

q: A nurse is preparing a patient with severe preeclampsia for possible emergency cesarean section. Which action is essential?
options: Ensure the patient has eaten a full meal.
Verify that consent is obtained, type and crossmatch is available, and magnesium sulfate is continued perioperatively.
Discontinue all IV fluids before surgery.
Remove the fetal monitor since it will not be needed.
rationale: Preparation for emergency cesarean in severe preeclampsia includes obtaining informed consent, ensuring blood products are available (due to hemorrhage risk, especially in HELLP), and continuing magnesium sulfate through the perioperative period for seizure prophylaxis.

q: A patient with preeclampsia without severe features at 36 weeks asks if she can take ibuprofen for her headache. The nurse's best response is:
options: No, NSAIDs such as ibuprofen are generally avoided in the third trimester and especially with hypertensive disorders; acetaminophen is a safer alternative.
Yes, ibuprofen is safe at any time during pregnancy.
Yes, but only if you take it with food.
rationale: NSAIDs like ibuprofen are generally avoided in the third trimester due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus, reduced amniotic fluid, and potential effects on platelet function. In preeclampsia, they may also worsen renal function. Acetaminophen is the recommended alternative.

q: Which nursing intervention is most appropriate for a patient with preeclampsia who is on strict intake and output monitoring?
options: Encourage unlimited oral fluid intake to flush the kidneys.
Administer large-volume IV boluses to improve urine output.
Restrict all oral and IV fluids.
Maintain IV fluids at a rate ordered by the provider, typically around 80-125 mL/hour, and monitor for signs of fluid overload.
rationale: In preeclampsia, fluid management must be carefully balanced. IV fluids are typically given at a moderate rate (80-125 mL/hr) because excessive fluids can cause pulmonary edema due to capillary leak, while insufficient fluids can worsen renal compromise.

q: A patient who had preeclampsia during her first pregnancy asks about her risk in a future pregnancy. The nurse correctly states:
options: Preeclampsia never recurs in subsequent pregnancies.
Preeclampsia only occurs in first pregnancies.
The risk decreases to zero if she waits 5 years.
There is an increased risk of recurrence, and she should discuss preconception planning including low-dose aspirin prophylaxis with her provider.
rationale: A history of preeclampsia is a significant risk factor for recurrence in subsequent pregnancies. The recurrence rate varies but can be 15-65% depending on the severity and gestational age of the prior episode. Low-dose aspirin starting at 12-16 weeks is recommended for prevention.

q: The nurse is monitoring a patient with chronic hypertension at 18 weeks. The patient's blood pressure today is 118/72 mmHg, lower than her pre-pregnancy baseline of 145/95 mmHg. The nurse understands this is due to:
options: Normal physiologic decrease in blood pressure during the second trimester due to decreased systemic vascular resistance.
The patient secretly taking additional antihypertensive medication.
rationale: During the second trimester, blood pressure normally decreases due to progesterone-mediated vasodilation and decreased systemic vascular resistance. This physiologic change can mask chronic hypertension and may lead to dosage adjustments but does not indicate resolution of the condition.

q: A 40-year-old patient with chronic hypertension and a BMI of 38 is in her first trimester. The nurse should anticipate that the provider will recommend all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Baseline laboratory assessment including renal and hepatic function
Increased surveillance with serial growth ultrasounds later in pregnancy
Initiation of an ACE inhibitor for better blood pressure control
rationale: ACE inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenic effects. All other listed interventions are appropriate for managing chronic hypertension in pregnancy, including aspirin prophylaxis, baseline labs, and serial growth monitoring.

q: A patient at 25 weeks with chronic hypertension develops new proteinuria (400 mg/24 hours). Her blood pressure remains at her baseline of 140/88 mmHg. The nurse recognizes this as:
rationale: The development of new-onset proteinuria (>= 300 mg/24 hours) in a patient with chronic hypertension after 20 weeks gestation meets the diagnostic criteria for superimposed preeclampsia. Without severe features (severe BP, thrombocytopenia, etc.), it is classified as without severe features.

q: A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving magnesium sulfate and labetalol. She reports difficulty breathing and the nurse auscultates bilateral crackles. The nurse suspects:
options: Pulmonary edema, a complication of severe preeclampsia
rationale: Pulmonary edema is a serious complication of severe preeclampsia caused by increased capillary permeability, decreased oncotic pressure, and fluid overload. Bilateral crackles with dyspnea in this clinical context should prompt immediate intervention including oxygen, upright positioning, and possible diuresis.
